title: How Much Signal Is in Early Training Trajectories? A Matched-Budget Study of Pseudo-Group Inference
description: This project is a controlled empirical study of pseudo-group signal quality for annotation-free robustness under spurious correlations. It asks whether short early-training trajectories, augmented with a lightweight trajectory-shape encoder, yield better pseudo-group fidelity than output-only early statistics or warmup-end features when warmup compute is matched.
motivation: Recent work suggests that annotation-free robustness is limited more by pseudo-group inference quality than by the downstream robust objective, but the literature does not appear to contain an exact matched-budget comparison between output-only early signals, richer multi-epoch trajectories, and warmup-end features under the same downstream learner. Because the individual ingredients already exist in prior work, the contribution should be framed honestly as a careful empirical benchmark with one lightweight new trajectory-aware descriptor rather than as a new robustness method.
proposed_approach: Warm up a pretrained classifier for 2 or 4 epochs and extract one of three within-class clustering signals: output-only early trajectories, richer trajectories that additionally include augmentation disagreement and feature drift, or the final warmup feature vector. For the temporal signals, apply a new lightweight trajectory-shape encoder that summarizes each scalar sequence by its start, end, mean, variance, net change, slope, and first-correct epoch, then cluster within class using a GMM to obtain soft pseudo-groups. Evaluate those pseudo-groups under the same weighted cross-group supervised contrastive fine-tuning stage, and also under a simple soft pseudo-group reweighted ERM control to show whether the signal-quality ranking transfers beyond contrastive learning.

related_work.title: Identifying Spurious Biases Early in Training through the Lens of Simplicity Bias
related_work.how_it_differs: SPARE is the closest baseline because it uses early outputs to expose spurious structure. The proposed study keeps warmup cost matched and asks whether extra multi-epoch information improves pseudo-group fidelity beyond those output-only signals.

related_work.title: Beyond Distribution Shift: Spurious Features Through the Lens of Training Dynamics
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper motivates the use of training dynamics by showing that spurious-feature learning leaves temporal signatures, but it is not a matched-budget pseudo-group inference benchmark. The proposed work turns that observation into a controlled signal-comparison study.

related_work.title: ExMap: Leveraging Explainability Heatmaps for Unsupervised Group Robustness to Spurious Correlations
related_work.how_it_differs: ExMap derives pseudo-groups from explainability heatmaps produced by a trained model. The proposed work is narrower and cheaper, using only short warmup trajectories and matched downstream training to isolate signal quality.

related_work.title: Improving Group Robustness on Spurious Correlation Requires Preciser Group Inference
related_work.how_it_differs: GIC argues that precise pseudo-group inference is the central bottleneck. The proposed study operationalizes that claim by varying only the signal used for group inference while keeping warmup budget and downstream learner fixed.

related_work.title: Learning Robust Classifiers with Self-Guided Spurious Correlation Mitigation
related_work.how_it_differs: This paper is part of the broader annotation-free robustness literature combining pseudo labels with robust training. The proposed work does not introduce a new mitigation objective; it studies which early-training signal provides the best pseudo-groups.

related_work.title: Correct-N-Contrast: A Contrastive Approach for Improving Robustness to Spurious Correlations
related_work.how_it_differs: Correct-N-Contrast motivates the contrastive downstream stage, but the proposed work is not another contrastive method paper. It treats xg-SupCon as a fixed downstream consumer of pseudo-group signal and checks whether the same signal ranking holds under a simple non-contrastive weighted ERM control.

hypothesis: At equal warmup cost, richer early-training trajectories with a lightweight trajectory-shape encoding yield more faithful pseudo-groups than output-only early statistics, especially under short warmup budgets such as 2 epochs. If that fidelity gain is real, it should improve worst-group accuracy not only for a fixed cross-group supervised contrastive learner but also for a simple soft pseudo-group reweighted ERM objective.

confirm: On Waterbirds, trajectory-based pseudo-groups beat output-only pseudo-groups on at least one fidelity metric such as NMI or minority-group F1 at both 2-epoch and 4-epoch warmup budgets.
The trajectory advantage is larger at 2 warmup epochs than at 4 warmup epochs, supporting the matched-budget short-warmup claim.
Under both weighted xg-SupCon and soft pseudo-group reweighted ERM, the trajectory signal yields better worst-group accuracy than the output-only signal across 3 seeds on Waterbirds.
The conclusion remains directionally unchanged under a cheap `K`-sensitivity analysis that compares fixed `K=2` with BIC-selected `K` in `{2,3,4}`.
refute: Output-only early-training signals perform as well as or better than trajectories on both pseudo-group fidelity and downstream robustness once warmup compute is matched.
Warmup-end feature clustering matches the temporal signals, suggesting that early trajectories add little useful information.
The signal ranking appears only under xg-SupCon and disappears under weighted ERM, indicating that the result is objective-specific rather than signal-specific.
Results are too unstable across seeds to support a defensible conclusion or depend on oracle group labels for cluster count, checkpoint choice, or hyperparameter selection.
